title: Alien Hunt: The Oumuamua Mystery
story: A strange visitor from beyond our solar system sparked excitement in 2017. Scientists spotted Oumuamua, a cigar-shaped object zooming through space. Its weird shape and speed made some wonder: could it be alien technology? On December 13, researchers began listening for radio signals from Oumuamua. While they found no signs of aliens, the hunt sparked imaginations worldwide. Later studies suggested it was likely just an oddly shaped chunk of rock from a distant planet.

activity_choices: Sent a probe to touch it
Tried to catch it with a net
follow_up_question: If you could name the next interstellar object discovered, what would you call it?

title: Farewell to the Baiji Dolphin
story: Splashing through China's Yangtze River, the Baiji dolphin once delighted onlookers with its playful antics. But in 2006, on December 13, scientists made a sad announcement. After an extensive search, they couldn't find any Baiji left. This gentle creature, also known as the Chinese River Dolphin, was declared extinct. It was a wake-up call to protect our rivers and the amazing animals that call them home.

title: Abel Tasman's Kiwi Discovery
story: Sailing through uncharted waters, Dutch explorer Abel Tasman spotted a mysterious land on December 13, 1642. His eyes widened as he gazed upon the rugged coastline of what we now call New Zealand's South Island. Tasman first named it Staten Landt, thinking it might connect to a place near South America. A year later, he changed his mind and dubbed it Nieuw Zeeland, inspired by a Dutch province.

title: Sweet Success: HARIBO's Gummy Beginning
story: In a small kitchen in Bonn, Germany, Hans Riegel had a sweet idea. He mixed sugar, starch, and fruit flavoring to create chewy candies. On December 13, 1920, Riegel turned this tasty experiment into a company called HARIBO. The name cleverly combined his own - HAns RIegel - with his hometown, BOnn. Little did he know his sugary creations would soon make the world smile!
activity_question: What does the name HARIBO stand for?
bonus_fact: HARIBO's famous gummy bears weren't invented until 1922!

title: Stargazers Measure a Giant's Waistline
story: Betelgeuse, a twinkling red giant in the night sky, had astronomers scratching their heads. How big was it really? On December 13, 1920, Francis G. Pease and his team at Mount Wilson Observatory found the answer. Using a clever device called an interferometer, they became the first to measure a star's size. Betelgeuse turned out to be enormous - if it replaced our sun, it would swallow up several planets!

follow_up_question: Can you spot Betelgeuse in the night sky? (Hint: It's in the constellation Orion!)

Haynes played with the group The Three Flames and later acted on stage and screen. He appeared in famous productions like 'The Wiz' and 'In the Heat of the Night'.
activity_question: What instrument did George 'Tiger' Haynes play?
follow_up_question: Can you name one of the famous productions Haynes acted in?

title: A Hymn Writer's Humble Beginnings
story: Phillips Brooks, the man behind the beloved Christmas carol 'O Little Town of Bethlehem', took his first breath in Boston on December 13, 1835. This future Episcopal bishop would grow up to touch hearts with his words and music, leaving a lasting legacy in the world of hymns.
activity_question: What famous Christmas carol did Phillips Brooks compose?
bonus_fact: Phillips Brooks later became an Episcopal bishop, combining his love for music with his religious calling.

title: Artist and Explorer Born in Vermont
story: Russell W. Porter came into the world in Springfield, Vermont in 1871. This talented American would grow up to become both an artist and an explorer. Porter's adventures took him to Alaska, where he used his artistic skills to capture the beauty of the frozen landscape. His unique blend of art and exploration made him a fascinating figure in American history.
activity_question: Where was Russell W. Porter born?
follow_up_question: What two careers did Russell W. Porter have?
